1: 'And I said: Hear, you heads of Jacob and rulers of the house of Israel! Is it not for you to know justice?' Micah directly addresses the political leadership — the roshei Ya'akov (heads of Jacob) and qetzinei beit Yisrael (rulers of Israel). These are the judges, elders, and tribal leaders whose primary responsibility is the administration of justice (mishpat). Micah's charge is not economic complaint but legal dereliction: those appointed to know justice have abandoned it.
2: 'You who hate the good and love the evil, who tear the skin from off my people and their flesh from off their bones.' The inversion of good and evil — hating what should be loved, loving what should be hated — is the fundamental moral disorder. The predatory imagery of flaying skin from bones is extreme and deliberate: Micah wants to shock his audience into recognizing the violence in what looks like ordinary legal and economic activity. 'Tearing skin from bone' describes judicial and economic extraction that leaves people stripped of everything.
3: 'Who eat the flesh of my people, and flay their skin from off them, and break their bones in pieces and chop them up like meat in a pot, like flesh in a cauldron.' The extended cannibal butchery metaphor continues. The cooking pot and cauldron were everyday domestic objects — Micah transforms them into images of the elite consuming the poor. The phrase 'flesh of my people' (she'er ammi) is used throughout: the rulers feed on YHWH's own covenant people.
4: 'Then they will cry to YHWH, but he will not answer them; he will hide his face from them at that time, because they have made their deeds evil.' The hiding of YHWH's face (yaster panav mehem) is the covenant curse of abandonment (Deut 31:17-18; Ps 13:1; Isa 59:2). The leadership that provided no justice in the courts will receive no justice from the divine court — their cry will be answered with silence. The retributive symmetry is precise.
5: 'Thus says YHWH concerning the prophets who lead my people astray, who cry “Peace” when they have something to eat, but declare war against him who puts nothing in their mouths.' The false prophets operate on a pay-for-prophecy model: feed them and they prophesy shalom (peace); withhold their fee and they declare holy war (qiddeshu milchamah) against you. This is the commercialization of prophecy that Micah condemns throughout — oracular output determined by economic incentive rather than divine mandate.
6: 'Therefore it shall be night to you, without vision, and darkness to you, without divination. The sun shall go down on the prophets, and the day shall be black over them.' The solar imagery is ironic: prophets are those who receive divine light/vision; their punishment is divine darkness — the withdrawal of the prophetic gift. The 'going down of the sun' on the prophets may also glance at Amos 8:9 ('I will make the sun go down at noon') as a shared Day-of-the-LORD image.
7: 'The seers shall be disgraced, and the diviners put to shame; they shall all cover their lips, for there is no answer from God.' 'Covering the lips' (atam al-saphah) was the gesture of a leper announcing his uncleanness (Lev 13:45) or of mourning (Ezek 24:17). The false prophets will be publicly shamed: their mouths, which spoke purchased prophecies, will be covered in shame before the absence of authentic divine speech. The contrast with Micah himself (v. 8) is direct.
8: 'But as for me, I am filled with power, with the Spirit of YHWH, and with justice and might, to declare to Jacob his transgression and to Israel his sin.' Micah's counter-claim to the false prophets: he speaks by the Spirit of YHWH, not for hire. The triple endowment — koach (power), mishpat (justice/judgment), gevurah (might) — contrasts with the darkness and shame decreed for the fee-prophets. His function is unambiguous: to 'declare to Jacob his transgression' — the very thing the false prophets refused to do.
9: 'Hear this, you heads of the house of Jacob and rulers of the house of Israel, who detest justice and make crooked all that is straight.' The final oracle of ch. 3 addresses all three corrupt groups together: rulers (vv. 9-10), priests (v. 11), and prophets (v. 11). They 'detest justice' (mete'avim mishpat) and make crooked what is straight (ha'et hayyeshaw ye'aqqeshu) — the exact inverse of their calling. Ps 9:7-8 and Isa 5:20 use similar language of good-evil inversion.
10: 'Who build Zion with blood and Jerusalem with iniquity.' The building of Jerusalem with blood (bedamim) echoes Hab 2:12 ('woe to him who builds a town with blood'). The magnificent building projects that adorned the capital city — the monuments of Israelite civilization — are built on systemic injustice and exploitation. The architecture that symbolizes the covenant people's prosperity is indicted as evidence of their crime.
11: 'Its heads give judgment for a bribe; its priests teach for a price; its prophets practice divination for money; yet they lean on YHWH and say, “Is not YHWH in the midst of us? No disaster shall come upon us.”' The three corrupt offices (judiciary, priesthood, prophecy) are all monetized. Most devastating: they invoke YHWH's presence as their security guarantee — the temple theology that says YHWH dwelling in Zion makes Jerusalem inviolable. This is the theology Jeremiah will combat in his famous Temple Sermon (Jer 7:4: 'the temple of YHWH, the temple of YHWH, the temple of YHWH').
12: 'Therefore because of you Zion shall be plowed as a field; Jerusalem shall become a heap of ruins, and the mountain of the house a wooded height.' Micah 3:12 is the most explicit announcement of Jerusalem's destruction in the pre-exilic prophets. So striking was this oracle that it was cited verbatim a century later as a precedent in Jeremiah's trial (Jer 26:18-19): Hezekiah did not execute Micah for saying this; therefore Jeremiah should not be executed either. The text thus provides rare biblical evidence for the citation and preservation of earlier prophetic tradition in legal/political debate.
